payload.title: File-drop inbox intake should use dated numbered files or folders as intent messages
payload.summary: Correction to the human file-drop inbox proposal: the intake item should be a filesystem item named in the form YYYYMMDD-NNN-slug. It may be a text/Markdown file, or it may be a folder. A folder should count as an intent message too, not merely as a container for other messages.
evidence: The operator clarified that Narada should accept both files and folders as human-created intent messages. The folder case is important because some human intentions need a small bundle of material: a README/body, evidence files, screenshots, exported logs, or draft artifacts. The folder's name and body file together identify one candidate message.
proposal: Revise canonical file-drop intake around numbered intake items. Accept items matching YYYYMMDD-NNN-slug as candidate messages. For files, parse the file body plus optional front matter. For folders, treat the folder itself as one candidate message; parse a canonical body file such as README.md, message.md, or intent.md when present, and treat remaining child files as attachments/evidence/supporting material. Admission should create exactly one canonical inbox envelope per intake item, with source.kind=file_drop and source.ref pointing to the file or folder path. Repeated scans must be idempotent by stable item identity and content hash.
payload.recommendation: Use the dated numbered name as the human-facing ordering and anti-collision convention. Dry-run should list every candidate item, whether file or folder, with parsed title/kind/authority, supporting-file count for folders, and admit/reject reason. This supersedes any interpretation that only .md/.txt files are accepted.
